item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually think this one is kinda bad in a low-key way. My guess is just that the repercussions of such a situation weren't completely thought through. This thing is a huge, widespread containment issue, because the effects would be observable to all the normal folk in the world in some VERY obvious ways, especially as time goes forward.

Notes: Webb’s lawyer, the fifth in her lifetime, missed a flight which later crashed, sparing no survivors.

Nobody is going to miss this. NOBODY. Everyone IN THE WORLD is going to find out about these people by the times you're listing in your case logs. You could maybe get away with this kind of thing pre 70's when it's starting, but by the time we hit 80's/90's, much less post millennium, this thing has literally no containment and all the normal governments know about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And I accidentally just misspelled "ensured" as "insured". Damn my pun-prone mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You mean that an island full of people like this should just get… Lost?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wish I'd thought of that! I think it's better if the audience imagines at least some of the outcomes, though.

That said, if the Foundation tried that, everybody responsible for insuring the island was kept off the map would suffer the consequences.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm fully with you in wanting to see what would happen with an isolated colony of -B instances.

I'm imaging that if they were on a remote, desolate and otherwise deserted island, they would begin to see an alarming number of planes and cruise ships constantly crashing into the island or running aground. Stocked with supplies, but always devoid of survivors.

Or, if the insurance provider were to cancel their policies after a number of years of this type of isolation due to increasing cost.

Now, more than anything, I want to know what would cause 2647 to raise the monthly costs for -B instances or cancel their policies completely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Added an addendum to reflect those concerns.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this idea and was going to upvote. However, I'm not sure how I feel about the Foundation forcing people who are already covered under the insurance to end their policies. I'm assuming they did not know what they were getting into when they signed up and essentially sentencing a lot of them to a particular gruesome death does not sit right with me and seems unnecessarily cruel of the Foundation.

I get that the goal is to prevent the negative side effects of the insurance from affecting other people but you specify that regular interaction with the -B instance is necessary for these to occur. Two possible, more humane solutions come to mind for me. Either stick them in complete Isolation and have their needs like food etc. be met automatically. Of course there is an argument to be made for unending isolation being a fate that's possibly even worse than death. A different way to go would be to have a changing roster of D-Class provide their needs and some social interaction. Switch the D-Class out often enough so they don't suffer the side effects.

A third interesting option would be to see what happens when, instead of forcing them to cancel their policies, you make them move into an isolated "colony" of -B instances. Their respective policies should prevent the side effects from affecting the other inhabitants and they could possibly live an almost normal life.

For now, I'm sticking with a novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, I've edited and rephrased some of what you suggested.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Looks like a word is missing in the first testing log. "Reginald's second [wife] survived a mugging."

I don't know how I feel about "created" as the term for someone becoming a -B instance. "Policy enacted at age XXX" seems a little more in keeping with the insurance bent. Likewise "incurred while holding the policy" seems off compared to "incurred while the policy is active". I work in insurance and it's possible that what I think of as appropriate insurance language is a little skewed by how we communicate internally; I am not suggesting that I know what the proper clinical phrase would be.

Overall, no vote for now. Small pieces of language that borders on non-clinical ("shrieked, panicked") and the aforementioned insurance phrasing keep me from buying 100% into the writing and the idea doesn't have any super squicky implications in the log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Effects pertaining to malnutrition, such as obesity…"

Obesity doesn't usually result from malnutrition. Maybe, "Effects pertaining to eating habits, such as…"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not bad. Not half bad at all. Pretty straightforward yes, but the logs and imagery are A+.
